summary: The post argues that fast pandas DataFrame construction is hampered by pandas’s complex internal block structure and dtype behavior around nulls. Even when input is already in pandas-friendly arrays, DataFrame creation incurs a costly consolidation copy. Arrow’s columnar format represents nulls more precisely, so zero-copy conversion to pandas requires careful construction of pandas’s native blocks. Recent Arrow work enables high-bandwidth conversion back to pandas, approaching memory bandwidth limits with multithreading. The conclusion is that Arrow can serve as a high-speed interchange layer for the Python ecosystem, while the pandas 2.0 roadmap aims to simplify memory representation and further reduce conversion overhead.
key_points: Pandas DataFrame construction is slowed by dtype changes with nulls and the internal consolidation into blocks.
Arrow’s separate null bitmap makes zero-copy conversion to pandas nontrivial despite its columnar design.
New Arrow-to-pandas conversion builds pandas’s internal blocks directly, yielding 6–10 GB/s throughput.
Multithreading significantly improves conversion speed and can approach hardware memory bandwidth.
Simplifying pandas’s memory model is a key goal for better interoperability with Arrow and other systems.
money_quotes: Constructing the precise block structure is the only true way to do zero-copy DataFrame construction.
For those who are counting, that's 7.58 GB/second just to do an internal memory copy.
One of my major goals in working on Arrow is to use it as a high-bandwidth IO pipe for the Python ecosystem.
At 9.71 GB/s, this is not far from saturating the main memory bandwidth on my consumer desktop hardware.
Note that numeric data is a best-case scenario; string or binary data would come with additional overhead while pandas continues to use Python objects in its memory representation.
By having high speed conversion to pandas, we can pay a small conversion cost to obtain a fully-fledged pandas DataFrame.
A simpler memory representation will also make it easier for other applications to interact with pandas at a low-level.
key_insight: High-throughput Arrow-to-pandas conversion is now fast enough to make Arrow a practical interchange layer, but pandas’s internal memory model remains the main barrier to true zero-copy interoperability.
